title: A Computable Functor from Graphs to Fields
overall_summary: The paper constructs a fully faithful functor F from the category of symmetric irreflexive graphs with induced-subgraph embeddings to the category of fields with field homomorphisms. The construction codes graph vertices and edges into function fields of carefully chosen high-genus algebraic curves whose rational-map rigidity lets one recover the graph, and in particular its automorphism group, from the field. For countable structures with domain ω, the authors refine the construction to a type-2 computable equivalence between graphs on ω and the essential image of the functor among fields on ω, including computable transformations of both objects and morphisms. This is directly useful for transferring graph-based computable-model-theoretic constructions to fields, especially constructions involving automorphisms, automorphism spectra, relative computability of automorphisms, and definability/interpretability phenomena.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Definition 3.1 and Definition 3.4: computable functors and computable equivalence of categories
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let C and C′ be categories whose objects are first-order structures with domain ω in computable languages, and whose morphisms are maps between these domains. A functor F:C→C′ is computable if there are Turing functionals Φ and Ψ such that, for every object S of C, Φ with oracle the atomic diagram of S computes the atomic diagram of F(S), and, for every morphism g:S→T in C, Ψ with oracle S⊕g⊕T computes the morphism F(g):F(S)→F(T). If F1,F2:C→C′ are computable functors, a computable natural transformation τ:F1→F2 is one for which a Turing functional computes τ(S):F1(S)→F2(S) uniformly from S. A computable equivalence of categories between C and C′ consists of computable functors F:C→C′ and G:C′→C together with computable isomorphisms of functors G∘F≅1_C and F∘G≅1_C′.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: These are definitions, not theorems. The important technical choice is that morphism computation is type-2: the oracle includes the source structure, the morphism as a function on ω, and the target structure. This is used later because the image of a field homomorphism, for example, can be computed only with access to the field operations and the relevant presentations of source and target.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Appendix A: algebraic-geometry facts used in the coding
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let k be a field, V and W varieties over k, and W integral. Lemma A.1 says V(k(W)) is naturally in bijection with rational maps W⇢V. Let k have characteristic 0, and let C and D be geometrically integral curves over k with equal genus g_C=g_D>1. Lemma A.2 says every nonconstant rational map C⇢D is birational. Let V1,...,Vn be geometrically integral k-varieties over a characteristic-0 field k, and let C be a geometrically integral k-curve of genus greater than 1. Lemma A.3 says every rational map V1×...×Vn⇢C factors through one of the projections V1×...×Vn→Vi.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For Lemma A.1, a k(W)-point of V is given by finitely many rational functions on W satisfying the equations of V, hence is regular on some dense open subset of W; conversely a rational map gives such a point. Lemma A.2 is the standard Hurwitz formula argument: a nonconstant map between smooth projective models of curves of the same genus greater than 1 must have degree 1, hence is birational. Lemma A.3 is proved by induction on n, reducing to n=2 and base-changing to an algebraically closed field. A rational map V1×V2⇢C is viewed as an algebraic family, parametrized by V2, of rational maps V1⇢C. By the de Franchis-Severi theorem, there are only finitely many nonconstant rational maps V1⇢C, so such maps cannot vary in an algebraic family. Therefore either the family is constantly one nonconstant map, so the original map factors through V1, or all maps in the family are constant, so the original map factors through V2.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 4.1: rigid curves X and Y_t with controlled rational points
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Define p(u,v)=u^4+16uv^3+10v^4+16v−4 in Q[u,v] and q(T,x,y)=x^4+y^4+1+T(x^4+xy^3+y+1) in Q[T,x,y]. Let X be the affine plane curve p(u,v)=0 over Q. For a field F and t in F, let Y_t be the affine plane curve q(t,x,y)=0 over F; let Y denote Y_T over Q(T). Then: (1) X and Y are geometrically integral; (2) g_X=g_Y>1, in fact both have genus 3; (3) after any base-field extension, neither X nor Y has a nontrivial birational automorphism; (4) after any base-field extension, Y is not birational to any curve definable over a finite extension of Q; (5) X(Q)=∅; (6) the u-coordinate map X(R)→R is surjective; (7) there is an open neighborhood U of 0 in R such that Y_t(R)=∅ for every t in U; and (8) if t∈[20,∞), then Y_t has a real point whose x-coordinate lies in [1,2].
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The projective closure of X reduces modulo 5 to u^4+uv^3+vw^3+w^4=0 over F_5. The projective closure of Y specializes at T=0 to x^4+y^4+z^4=0 and at T=∞ to x^4+xy^3+yz^3+z^4=0. A result of Poonen on hypersurfaces without extra automorphisms implies that the relevant reductions are smooth, projective, geometrically integral plane quartics of genus 3 with no nontrivial birational automorphisms after base extension; this gives geometric integrality, genus 3, and rigidity for X and Y. For (4), if Y were birational to a curve over a finite extension of Q, then the specializations at T=0 and T=∞ would become isomorphic over Q-bar; but the T=0 curve has a nontrivial automorphism (x,y,z)↦(−x,y,z), while the T=∞ curve is rigid, contradiction. For (5), the projective model of X modulo 8 is u^4+2v^4+4w^4=0, which has no point in P^2(Z/8Z), so X(Q)=∅. For (6), fix u∈R. Since p(u,v)→+∞ as v→+∞, it suffices to find v with p(u,v)<0. If |u|<√2, v=0 works; if |u|≥√2, v=−u gives p(u,−u)=−5u^4−16u−4<0. For (7), the compact real projective curve at T=0 has no real points, so nearby fibers have no real points. For (8), for t≥20 and x∈[1,2], one has q(t,x,−3)<0 and q(t,x,0)>0, so the intermediate value theorem gives y with q(t,x,y)=0.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Construction 5.A: the field F(G) attached to a graph G
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For a graph G=(V,E), let K=Q(∏_{i∈V} X), interpreted as the direct limit of function fields over finite products of copies of X. Let u_i,v_i∈K be the rational functions u,v on the i-th copy of X. For each unordered pair {i,j}⊆V, define a K-curve Z_{i,j} by Z_{i,j}=Y_{u_i u_j} if {i,j} is an edge of G, and Z_{i,j}=Y_{u_i+u_j} if {i,j} is not an edge. Define F(G)=K(∏_{ {i,j}∈(V choose 2)} Z_{i,j}), again as a direct limit over finite products. A graph morphism G→G′, i.e. an injection preserving and reflecting adjacency, sends each u_i,v_i and each edge/nonedge coding curve to the corresponding objects for G′, inducing a field homomorphism F(G)→F(G′). This defines a functor F from graphs to fields. If G is finite then F(G) is countable; if G is infinite then |F(G)|=|G|.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The construction first adds one independent copy of X for each vertex, so the vertex i is represented by the point (u_i,v_i). Then for each pair {i,j}, it adds one function-field point on exactly one of two possible curves: Y_{u_i u_j} encodes adjacency, and Y_{u_i+u_j} encodes non-adjacency. A graph morphism preserves the edge/nonedge dichotomy, so the same rational functions and curve equations are respected after applying the induced map on vertex indices. The cardinality assertion follows because adjoining function fields over finitely or countably many curves keeps a finite graph construction countable, while an infinite graph contributes one transcendence generator pair per vertex and no larger cardinality than the vertex set.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 5.2: no unwanted rational maps among the coding curves
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Fix a graph G=(V,E) and let K=Q(∏_{i∈V}X) as in Construction 5.A. For any field extension L of K, consider over L the base changes of X and all curves Y_{u_i u_j} and Y_{u_i+u_j} for unordered pairs {i,j}⊆V. The only nonconstant rational maps between any two curves in this list are the identity maps from a curve to itself.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: All these curves have the same genus greater than 1. By Lemma A.2, any nonconstant rational map between two of them would have to be birational. Lemma 4.1 gives that X and Y have no nontrivial birational automorphisms, so a nonconstant self-map is the identity. Lemma 4.1(4) rules out birational equivalence between X and a Y_t with t transcendental. If t and t′ are algebraically independent over Q and Y_t became birational to Y_t′ after base extension, one could specialize t′ to an algebraic value while keeping t transcendental, contradicting Lemma 4.1(4). The parameters u_i u_j and u_i+u_j occurring in the construction are distinct and have the needed algebraic independence properties, so no two distinct curves in the list become birational.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 5.3: field points of X and Y_t recover exactly the graph data
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let G=(V,E) be a graph and F(G) the field of Construction 5.A. For each pair {i,j}, let x_{ij},y_{ij} be the rational functions x,y on the curve Z_{i,j} adjoined in the construction. Then: (i) X(F(G)) consists exactly of the points (u_i,v_i) for i∈V. (ii) If {i,j}∈E, then Y_{u_i u_j}(F(G))={(x_{ij},y_{ij})} and Y_{u_i+u_j}(F(G))=∅. (iii) If {i,j}∉E, then Y_{u_i u_j}(F(G))=∅ and Y_{u_i+u_j}(F(G))={(x_{ij},y_{ij})}.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Because F(G) is the direct limit of fields K(Z) for finite products Z of the pair-coding curves, an F(G)-point of X corresponds by Lemma A.1 to a rational map from such a finite product Z to X_K. Lemmas A.3 and 5.2 force every such rational map to be constant, so X(F(G))=X(K). Now a K-point of X corresponds to a rational map from a finite product of vertex copies of X to X. Lemmas A.3 and 5.2 imply that such a map is either a projection to one of the factors or constant. A constant map would yield a Q-point of X, impossible by Lemma 4.1(5), so the only points are the coordinate points (u_i,v_i). For the Y-statements, the same rational-map analysis shows that a point on the matching curve arises only from the identity map on the corresponding adjoined Z_{i,j}, giving the single point (x_{ij},y_{ij}), while the nonmatching curve gets no such identity contribution. It remains to exclude K-rational points on either Y_{u_i u_j} or Y_{u_i+u_j}; using Lemma 4.1(6), embed K into R so that all u_i are algebraically independent real numbers sufficiently close to 0, and then Lemma 4.1(7) gives no real points on those Y_t, hence no K-points.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 6.1: recovering a graph from its coded field
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let E be the essential image of F in the category of fields. For F in E, define a graph G(F) as follows. Its vertex set is X(F). If i=(u_i,v_i) and j=(u_j,v_j) are two distinct vertices, put an edge between i and j exactly when Y_{u_i u_j}(F) has an F-point; Lemma 5.3 ensures that exactly one of Y_{u_i u_j}(F) and Y_{u_i+u_j}(F) has an F-point. For every graph G, the map ε_G:G→G(F(G)) sending i to (u_i,v_i) is a graph isomorphism, functorially in G. Thus G∘F≅1_Graphs.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Lemma 5.3(i) says that the vertices X(F(G)) are precisely the points (u_i,v_i), so ε_G is a bijection on vertices. Lemma 5.3(ii)-(iii) says that for a pair {i,j}, Y_{u_i u_j} has a point in F(G) exactly in the edge case, while Y_{u_i+u_j} has a point exactly in the nonedge case. Therefore ε_G preserves and reflects adjacency. Functoriality is immediate because graph morphisms send each vertex generator to the corresponding vertex generator in the target.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 6.2: the inverse graph functor is faithful on the essential image
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let E be the essential image of F. For fields F,F′ in E, the map Hom_E(F,F′)→Hom_Graphs(G(F),G(F′)) induced by the graph-recovery functor G is injective. Equivalently, a field homomorphism f:F→F′ in E is uniquely determined by its induced map X(F)→X(F′) on recovered graph vertices.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: After replacing F by an isomorphic field of the form F(G), the field F is generated by the vertex coordinates u_i,v_i and the pair-coding coordinates x_{ij},y_{ij}. The induced graph map G(f) gives f(u_i),f(v_i) for every vertex i. For an edge {i,j}, f must send (x_{ij},y_{ij})∈Y_{u_i u_j}(F) to an F′-point of Y_{f(u_i)f(u_j)}; by Lemma 5.3, that target point is unique. For a nonedge, the same argument uses Y_{u_i+u_j}. Therefore f is determined on all generators and hence on all of F.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 1.1: a fully faithful functor from graphs to fields
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let Graphs be the category of symmetric irreflexive graphs with morphisms the injections that are isomorphisms onto induced subgraphs, and let Fields be the category of fields with field homomorphisms. The functor F:Graphs→Fields of Construction 5.A is fully faithful. Thus for any graphs G and H, the induced map Hom_Graphs(G,H)→Hom_Fields(F(G),F(H)) is a bijection. In particular, Aut(F(G)) is naturally isomorphic to Aut(G).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Proposition 6.1 gives a functor G on the essential image of F with G∘F≅1_Graphs. Proposition 6.2 says G is faithful. To see fullness, let h:F(G)→F(H) be a field homomorphism. Applying G gives a graph morphism G(h):G(F(G))→G(F(H)), and using ε_G and ε_H from Proposition 6.1 this corresponds to a graph morphism G→H. The field homomorphism induced by this graph morphism has the same image under G as h, so Proposition 6.2 forces it to equal h. Faithfulness of F follows similarly from G∘F≅1. The automorphism-group statement is the special case G=H.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Section 7 construction: standard presentations F_ω(G) on domain ω
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For graphs G=(ω,E) with domain ω, the paper modifies F(G) to a field F_ω(G) whose domain is exactly ω. It partitions ω into computable infinite columns ω[n] using a standard pairing function. The first column is made into a computable copy of Q. Even-numbered columns are used uniformly to adjoin the vertex copies of X, producing generators u_i,v_i for each vertex i. Odd-numbered columns are used, in lexicographic order of pairs (i,j) with i>j, to adjoin the pair-coding curve Y_{u_i u_j} if {i,j} is an edge and Y_{u_i+u_j} if {i,j} is a nonedge. The resulting union field is F_ω(G), and the action on morphisms is the same as for F.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The underlying effective algebraic step is that, given a field k with domain a subset of ω and an irreducible polynomial f(x,y)∈k[x,y] of positive y-degree d, the function field k(x)[y]/(f) can be presented uniformly on a disjoint new copy of ω as k(x)⊕k(x)y⊕...⊕k(x)y^{d−1}. Iterating this construction over the computable column partition gives a uniform presentation on all of ω. The construction is arranged so that the pairing-code positions of the vertex points (u_i,v_i) increase with i, which is later used to identify the recovered vertex enumeration.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 7.1: computable vertex embedding into F_ω(G)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For each graph G∈Graphs_ω, let μ:G→F_ω(G) be the injection sending a vertex i to the field element u_i adjoined for that vertex. Then μ is computable uniformly, its range μ(G) is a computable subset of the domain F_ω(G)=ω, and μ^{-1} on μ(G) is computable. Moreover μ varies functorially with G.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The columns and the elements u_i are chosen by a uniform construction independent of the edge relation of G. Given a field-domain element j∈ω, one can determine effectively which column contains j. Only the vertex-adjoining columns can contain a u_i. On such a column, the construction lets one compute u_i and check whether j=u_i; if yes, i is recovered effectively. Since graph morphisms send vertex i to vertex g(i), the corresponding field morphisms send u_i to u_{g(i)}, giving functoriality.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemmas 7.2 and 7.3: uniform enumeration of recovered vertices
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For F in the essential image E_ω of F_ω among fields with domain ω, view X(F) as a subset of F×F=ω×ω and order it by the standard pairing function π:ω×ω→ω. Lemma 7.2 says that X(F) is infinite countable and there is an order-preserving bijection δ_F:ω→X(F) such that δ_F and δ_F^{-1} are computable uniformly from the atomic diagram of F. Lemma 7.3 says that if F=F_ω(G) is the canonical field built from a graph G, then the natural vertex map ε_G:i↦(u_i,v_i) is order-preserving, hence ε_G=δ_{F_ω(G)}.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: If F∈E_ω, then F≅F_ω(G) for some graph G, and Lemma 5.3 gives X(F_ω(G)) in bijection with the vertices of G, so X(F) is countably infinite. Given the field operations of F, one can enumerate pairs (a,b)∈ω×ω in increasing π-order and test p(a,b)=0, thereby enumerating X(F); the i-th discovered point is δ_F(i). This gives δ_F and its inverse relative to F. In the canonical construction, the vertex generators were deliberately placed so that π(u_i,v_i) increases with i, so ε_G is the same order-preserving enumeration.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 7.6: computing a field morphism from the recovered graph morphism
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: There is a Turing functional which, given fields F,F′ in E_ω and a graph morphism g:G_ω(F)→G_ω(F′), computes the unique field morphism f:F→F′ such that G_ω(f)=g.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Existence and uniqueness follow from full faithfulness. To compute f, first use δ_F and δ_F′ to compute the vertex coordinates (u_i,v_i)=δ_F(i) in F and their images δ_F′(g(i)) in F′. For each pair {i,j}, determine from G_ω(F) whether it is an edge. If it is an edge, the pair-coding point (x_{ij},y_{ij}) lies on Y_{u_i u_j}; if it is a nonedge, it lies on Y_{u_i+u_j}. Its image under f must be the unique point on the corresponding target curve with parameters f(u_i),f(u_j), and this point can be found by searching F′ because Lemma 5.3 guarantees uniqueness and existence. Finally, for an arbitrary z∈F, search for finitely many generators among the u_i,v_i,x_{ij},y_{ij} and a rational function expression for z in those generators; such an expression exists because these elements generate F. Evaluate the same rational function at the computed images to obtain f(z).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 1.2: computable equivalence between graphs on ω and the essential image in fields
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let Graphs_ω be the category of symmetric irreflexive graphs with domain ω and induced-subgraph embeddings, let Fields_ω be the category of fields with domain ω and field homomorphisms, and let E_ω be the full subcategory of Fields_ω consisting of fields isomorphic to F_ω(G) for some G∈Graphs_ω. Then F_ω:Graphs_ω→E_ω and the recovered-graph functor G_ω:E_ω→Graphs_ω are computable functors. Moreover G_ω∘F_ω is exactly the identity functor on Graphs_ω, and F_ω∘G_ω is computably isomorphic to the identity functor on E_ω.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For G∈Graphs_ω, the column-by-column construction computes the atomic diagram of F_ω(G) from the edge relation of G, giving the object part of F_ω. For F∈E_ω, compute δ_F by Lemma 7.2. To decide whether {i,j} is an edge of G_ω(F), compute δ_F(i)=(u_i,v_i) and δ_F(j)=(u_j,v_j), then search in parallel for an F-point on Y_{u_i u_j} and on Y_{u_i+u_j}; Lemma 5.3 ensures exactly one search succeeds, and which one succeeds determines edge versus nonedge. This computes the object part of G_ω. A field morphism f:F→F′ is sent by G_ω to δ_F′^{-1}∘f∘δ_F, computable from F⊕f⊕F′. Lemma 7.3 gives G_ω(F_ω(G))=G on objects, and the same functorial identification gives equality on morphisms. For the morphism part of F_ω, given computable access to graphs G,G′ and a graph morphism g:G→G′, use Proposition 7.6 with the fact that G_ω(F_ω(g))=g to compute F_ω(g). Finally, for F∈E_ω, Proposition 7.6 applied to the identity graph isomorphism G_ω(F_ω(G_ω(F)))=G_ω(F) computes mutually inverse field isomorphisms F_ω(G_ω(F))↔F, uniformly in F; this is the computable natural isomorphism F_ω∘G_ω≅1_Eω.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollaries 1.3 and 1.4: computing a graph presentation from any field copy in the image
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: If a field F∈Fields_ω is isomorphic to a field in the image of F_ω, then from the atomic diagram of F one can compute a graph G∈Graphs_ω and an isomorphism F→F_ω(G). In particular, every computable field isomorphic to a field in the image of F_ω is computably isomorphic to F_ω(G) for some computable graph G.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Take G=G_ω(F), which is computable from F by Theorem 1.2. The computable natural isomorphism F_ω∘G_ω≅1_Eω gives, uniformly from F, an isomorphism between F and F_ω(G) in one direction and its inverse in the other. If F is computable, then G_ω(F) is computable and the isomorphism is computable.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 1.5: degrees of graph presentations corresponding to a fixed field in the image
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let F∈E_ω, and let I={G∈Graphs_ω : F_ω(G)≅F}. Then: (a) I is exactly one graph isomorphism class in Graphs_ω. (b) Either every graph in I is automorphically trivial and computable, or there exists a graph in I whose Turing degree is equal to the Turing degree of F.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For (a), Theorem 1.2 gives a fully faithful equivalence between Graphs_ω and E_ω, so isomorphism of the fields F_ω(G) and F_ω(H) is equivalent to isomorphism of G and H. For (b), Corollary 1.3 gives some G∈I with G≤_T F. If G is automorphically trivial, then every graph in its isomorphism class is automorphically trivial; for such graphs, a finite set S_0 controls all structure, so whether {i,j} is an edge is determined by finitely many equalities involving S_0, and the graph is computable. If G is not automorphically trivial, Knight's theorem says the spectrum of G is upward closed under Turing reducibility. Since deg(G)≤deg(F), the degree deg(F) is in the spectrum of G, so there is an isomorphic copy G′∈I with deg(G′)=deg(F).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 9.1: Turing degrees of objects and morphisms are preserved
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For every graph G∈Graphs_ω, the atomic diagram of G and the atomic diagram of F_ω(G) are Turing equivalent: G≡_T F_ω(G). If g:G→G′ is a morphism between computable graphs in Graphs_ω, then g and the induced field homomorphism F_ω(g):F_ω(G)→F_ω(G′) are Turing equivalent: g≡_T F_ω(g).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Since F_ω is a computable functor, F_ω(G) is computable from G. Since G_ω is computable and G_ω(F_ω(G))=G, the graph G is computable from F_ω(G). This proves object-degree equivalence. If G and G′ are computable, then F_ω(g) is computable from g by the morphism part of the computable functor F_ω. Conversely, applying the computable functor G_ω to F_ω(g) recovers g because G_ω∘F_ω is the identity, so g is computable from F_ω(g).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 9.2: Turing degree spectra of graphs and their coded fields
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For G∈Graphs_ω, let Spec(M) denote the set of Turing degrees of presentations on domain ω isomorphic to M. If G is automorphically trivial, then Spec(G)={0}, while Spec(F_ω(G)) contains all Turing degrees. If G is not automorphically trivial, then Spec(G)=Spec(F_ω(G)).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: If G is automorphically trivial, every presentation of G is computable, so Spec(G)={0}. The field F_ω(G) is computable but is not automorphically trivial; Knight's upward-closure theorem for non-automorphically-trivial structures then implies that the spectrum of F_ω(G) contains every Turing degree. If G is not automorphically trivial, Proposition 9.1 applied to every copy G′≅G gives Spec(G)⊆Spec(F_ω(G)). Conversely, Proposition 1.5(b) applied to a field copy F≅F_ω(G) gives a graph copy in the same isomorphism class with the same degree as F, yielding Spec(F_ω(G))⊆Spec(G).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 9.7: degree spectra of relations transfer to fields
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let A be a computable structure that is not automorphically trivial, and let R be an n-ary relation on A, not necessarily in the language. The degree spectrum DgSp_A(R) is the set of Turing degrees of images of R under isomorphisms from A to computable copies of A. Then there is a computable field F and an n-ary relation S on F such that DgSp_A(R)=DgSp_F(S).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: By the graph-completeness theorem of Hirschfeldt, Khoussainov, Shore, and Slinko, replace A by a computable graph G with a relation R having the same relation-degree spectrum. Let F=F_ω(G), and let S=μ(R), applying the computable vertex embedding μ from Lemma 7.1 coordinatewise. If g:G→G′ is an isomorphism to a computable graph, then functoriality gives a commutative diagram with μ on the horizontal arrows and F_ω(g) on fields. The image R′ of R in G′ and the image S′ of S in F_ω(G′) are Turing equivalent because μ and μ^{-1} on its range are computable. Hence every degree arising from R arises from S. Conversely, if f:F→F′ is an isomorphism to a computable field copy, Corollary 1.4 supplies a computable graph G′ and a computable isomorphism F′→F_ω(G′); composing with this computable isomorphism does not change the degree of the image relation. By full faithfulness, the resulting field isomorphism is F_ω(g) for some graph isomorphism g:G→G′. The first half of the argument then shows that the degree of the image of S is the degree of the corresponding image of R.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 9.9: automorphism spectra transfer to fields
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For every computable structure A, there is a computable field F with the same automorphism spectrum as A, where the automorphism spectrum is the set of Turing degrees of nontrivial automorphisms of the structure.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper invokes the result of Harizanov, Miller, and Morozov, using the graph-coding theorem of Hirschfeldt, Khoussainov, Shore, and Slinko, that every automorphism spectrum is realized by a computable graph G. Then take F=F_ω(G). Full faithfulness of F_ω gives a bijection between automorphisms of G and automorphisms of F, and Proposition 9.1(b) says each automorphism and its image under F_ω have the same Turing degree when the underlying graph presentations are computable. Nontriviality is preserved by the faithful functor, so the automorphism spectra are equal.

proof_techniques.name: Rigid high-genus curve coding of graph structure into fields
proof_techniques.description: The construction represents each graph vertex by a point (u_i,v_i) on a copy of a carefully selected genus-3 curve X, and each pair of vertices by adjoining a point on one of two curves Y_t: the parameter t=u_i u_j codes an edge, while t=u_i+u_j codes a nonedge. The curves are chosen so that there are no unwanted rational maps among X and the various Y_t curves. This rigidity ensures that the only X-points in the resulting field are the intended vertex points and that exactly one of the two Y-curves for a pair has a field point.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If a candidate example for the original problem is built as a graph, this technique gives a field with the same automorphism group and tightly controlled automorphism correspondence. The edge/nonedge dichotomy by existential solvability of curves also gives a concrete interpretation of the graph inside the field.

proof_techniques.name: Full faithfulness as automorphism-group preservation
proof_techniques.description: The paper proves not merely that graph isomorphism types are encoded into fields, but that every field homomorphism between coded fields comes from a unique graph morphism. For automorphisms, this yields a natural group isomorphism Aut(G)≅Aut(F(G)), and the proof identifies automorphisms through their action on the definable set X(F(G)) of vertex points.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Properties quantified over automorphisms can be transferred through this functor if the relevant computability or definability notions are preserved by the interpretation. A nondefinable automorphism of a graph candidate should correspond to a unique automorphism of the coded field, with no extra field automorphisms to check.

proof_techniques.name: Type-2 computable functoriality for both objects and morphisms
proof_techniques.description: The authors formalize computability of a functor by requiring uniform Turing functionals for transforming atomic diagrams of objects and for transforming morphisms when given the source, morphism, and target as oracles. This is stronger than merely mapping computable structures to computable structures: it gives uniform control over noncomputable copies and over individual isomorphisms or automorphisms.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The original problem involves relative computability of automorphisms in arbitrary presentations, including on a cone. A type-2 computable equivalence is the right tool for transferring such relative automorphism-computability statements between graphs and fields or other coded classes.

proof_techniques.name: Computable inverse via enumerating a definable vertex set
proof_techniques.description: For a field F in the essential image, the inverse functor G_ω computes the graph by enumerating X(F) in the order induced by the standard pairing function on F×F. To decide adjacency between two recovered vertices, it searches in parallel for a field point on Y_{u_i u_j} and on Y_{u_i+u_j}; the rigidity lemma guarantees exactly one search terminates.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This gives a uniform way to pull an arbitrary field copy back to a graph copy. For on-a-cone arguments, one can analyze automorphisms or parameter tuples in the recovered graph and then push results back to the field.

proof_techniques.name: Unique-point forcing for computing morphisms
proof_techniques.description: Once a graph morphism between recovered graphs is known, the corresponding field morphism is computable because the images of vertex generators determine the parameters of each pair-coding curve, and Lemma 5.3 gives a unique target point on the appropriate curve. Arbitrary field elements are handled by searching for rational expressions in the generators and evaluating those expressions at the computed images.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This technique proves equality of Turing degrees for automorphisms under the coding, not just reducibility in one direction. It is especially relevant for showing that if every graph automorphism is computable relative to a presentation plus a cone oracle, then the corresponding field automorphism is too.

proof_techniques.name: Column-based standard presentations on ω
proof_techniques.description: The field F_ω(G) is built on a computable partition of ω into columns, with Q on the first column, vertex-function-field extensions on even columns, and pair-coding extensions on odd columns. This makes the presentation uniform and provides computable handles such as the map i↦u_i and its computable inverse on its range.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: When constructing a computable presentation intended to have delicate automorphism properties, this method separates coding regions and gives computable decoding maps. Such computable handles are useful when comparing finite parameter tuples in the original structure and its coded version.

proof_techniques.name: Relation-spectrum transfer using a functorial computable embedding of vertices
proof_techniques.description: Theorem 9.7 transfers degree spectra of arbitrary relations by mapping a graph relation R to μ(R) inside F_ω(G), where μ sends vertices to their corresponding field elements u_i. Functoriality ensures that images under graph isomorphisms correspond to images under field isomorphisms, and computability of μ and μ^{-1} on its range preserves Turing degrees.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The graph of an automorphism can be treated as a binary relation when studying its degree behavior under copies. This suggests a way to transfer not only automorphism groups but also complexity information about particular automorphisms.

proof_techniques.name: Effective interpretation viewpoint
proof_techniques.description: The paper notes that the graph G is effectively interpretable in F(G): the interpreted domain is the existentially definable set X(F(G)), and the edge relation is existentially definable by the existence of a point on Y_{u_i u_j}. It also notes that F(G) is effectively interpretable in G, less directly, and cites later work showing that computable functors correspond to uniform effective interpretability.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The original problem involves Σ^in_1-definability of automorphisms from finite parameters. Effective bi-interpretability is a plausible mechanism for translating definability or nondefinability of automorphism graphs between a graph construction and its field coding.

other_useful_info: Section 3.C explicitly connects this construction to effective interpretations: the graph domain inside the field is uniformly existentially definable by the curve X, and adjacency is uniformly existentially definable by the existence of a point on the appropriate Y-curve. The same section cites Harrison-Trainor, Melnikov, Miller, and Montalbán, Computable functors and effective interpretability, arXiv:1506.02737v1, for the general equivalence between computable functors and uniform effective interpretability. Remark 9.8 states that functoriality also transfers relative intrinsic definability classes of relations, including relatively intrinsically Σ^0_α, Π^0_α, and Σ^1_m, between a graph G and the corresponding field F_ω(G), and that every definable relation on G is mapped to a definable relation on F_ω(G) when the construction is viewed as a bi-interpretation. The paper relies on the graph-completeness theorem of Hirschfeldt, Khoussainov, Shore, and Slinko, Annals of Pure and Applied Logic 115 (2002), to transfer many computable-model-theoretic properties from arbitrary non-automorphically-trivial structures to graphs before applying F_ω.
why_relevant: Constructs computable functors preserving essential computable-model-theoretic properties, including automorphism spectra. This is useful for transferring any graph-based construction solving the problem into fields or other algebraic settings.
